summary: Hot second set, legendary Scarlet>Touch and tight jam flow
blurb: The show delivers a scorching Scarlet Begonias → Touch of Grey transition and an energetic second set that never loses momentum. Highlights include Kansas City, Stella Blue and a solid rhythm section. Overall the performance is vibrant and consistently strong throughout.
review: On October 28, 1985 at Atlanta’s Fox Theater the Dead turned in a high‑octane concert that still feels fresh. The first set built a steady groove with a soulful Kansas City and a plaintive Stella Blue that let Jerry’s voice sound “beautifully sorrowful” while Brent added his trademark vocal interplay. The second set ignited with the now‑mythic Scarlet Begonias → Touch of Grey segue, which multiple listeners called a fire‑starter, followed by a seamless flow through Man Smart/Woman Smarter, Terrapin Station and an expansive Jam/Drums/Space stretch that showcased Mickey and Billy’s tight, “cowbell‑filled” drumming. Brent’s keyboards cooked Kansas City and delivered a standout solo on Ramble On Rose, while Bob Weir’s vocals rose to Allman‑like heights. Though the soundboard capture has a slight treble tilt and occasional clipping, the energy and musicality make this night one of ’85’s highlights.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Ten of thirteen recordings are strongly positive, repeatedly praising the Scarlet>Touch transition, high‑energy second set, and tight band playing. No consistent negative comments appear, so a robust +1.8 sentiment boost reflects the widespread acclaim.

best_recording.reason: Good soundboard capture with clear instrument separation; despite slight treble‑high tilt and occasional clipping, it conveys the show’s energy best of all available sources.

key_highlights: Legendary Scarlet Begonias → Touch of Grey transition that ignites the second set
Tight rhythm section – Mickey and Billy lock in with extra cowbell energy
Brent Mydland’s keyboard work shines on Kansas City and Ramble On Rose solo
Consistently strong performances of Kansas City, Stella Blue and Terrapin Station

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s guitar is haunting on Stella Blue and generally solid throughout; his vocals are described as beautifully sorrowful, showing he was ‘waking up’ in this era.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s vocals break out the stops on Round → Good Lovin', deliver Allman‑like harmonies on Cassidy, and his rhythm work is praised for hot, off‑beat strumming that adds charm to the set.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland provides vocal interplay (especially on Women Are Smarter), cooks Kansas City with “falling keys,” and delivers a superb solo on Ramble On Rose; overall his keyboards drive the energy of the night.
band_performance.Mickey: Drums are ‘really good,’ syncing tightly with Billy and adding extra cowbell power during the Rhythm Devils segment.
band_performance.Billy: Provides solid, tight drumming that locks in with Mickey, helping keep the band on all cylinders.
